content.text: The Quadratic Wall

Full self-attention computes a score between every pair of positions, producing an T×T matrix. Memory scales as O(T²): at T=4096 with 32 heads and float16, the attention matrices alone occupy ~8 GB per layer. For long-document tasks (legal, scientific, code), T easily reaches 32k–128k — at which point O(T²) memory becomes infeasible on any current hardware without approximation.
Sliding-window attention (SWA) reduces this to O(T·w) by restricting each token to attend only to the
w
nearest past tokens, where w is the window size. For T=32k and w=4096, SWA uses 8× less attention memory than full attention.

attrs.alt: Full causal attention grid versus sliding-window attention with window w=3, showing active cells highlighted in each approach

content.text: Local Attention and the Receptive Field
With a window of size w, token t sees directly only tokens
. But across L transformer layers, information can propagate further: after k layers, a token's effective receptive field spans
k·w
positions. This is analogous to how a deep CNN with small convolutions still has a wide effective receptive field.

attrs.latex: \text{receptive field after } L \text{ layers} = L \times w

content.text: For Mistral 7B (L=32, w=4096), the top-layer receptive field is 32×4096 = 131,072 tokens — sufficient to cover the entire context window of 32k. Long-range dependencies are bridged by propagating information layer-by-layer through overlapping windows.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

def sliding_window_attention(q, k, v, window: int):
    """
    Causal sliding-window attention.
    q, k, v: (B, T, d_k)
    Attends to at most `window` past tokens (including self).
    """
    B, T, d_k = q.shape
    # Build the full T×T score matrix, then mask everything outside the window
    scores = torch.bmm(q, k.transpose(1, 2)) / math.sqrt(d_k)  # (B, T, T)

    # Causal mask: upper triangle blocked
    causal = torch.tril(torch.ones(T, T, device=q.device, dtype=torch.bool))
    # Window mask: positions more than `window` steps back are also blocked
    window_mask = torch.ones(T, T, device=q.device, dtype=torch.bool)
    for i in range(T):
        window_mask[i, :max(0, i - window + 1)] = False

    mask = causal & window_mask
    scores = scores.masked_fill(~mask, float('-inf'))
    attn = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
    attn = torch.nan_to_num(attn)   # softmax(-inf, …, -inf) → nan; replace with 0
    return torch.bmm(attn, v)

# Quick test
B, T, d_k, w = 2, 16, 32, 4
q = torch.randn(B, T, d_k)
out = sliding_window_attention(q, q, q, window=w)
print(out.shape)   # (2, 16, 32)

# Receptive field grows linearly with depth
for n_layers in [1, 4, 8, 16, 32]:
    field = n_layers * w
    print(f'{n_layers:2d} layers × w={w}: field={field} tokens')
# 1 layers: field=4
# 32 layers: field=128  (covers full 128-token context)
Efficient Implementation: Block-Sparse CUDA Kernels
The naive approach above still materialises the full T×T score matrix and masks most of it — memory is not actually saved. Efficient SWA requires a
block-sparse or chunked kernel
that only computes the diagonal band. FlashAttention-2 and its extensions support this: the attention matrix is processed in tiles, and tiles outside the window band are skipped entirely.

Mistral 7B uses SWA with w=4096 implemented via a rolling buffer KV cache at inference: older keys and values are evicted once they fall outside the window, keeping the cache size constant at w positions regardless of generation length.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

# Mistral 7B uses sliding-window attention with w=4096
# The model handles this transparently — no special API needed
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1')
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    'mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1',
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map='auto',
)
# Check the configured window size
print(model.config.sliding_window)          # 4096
print(model.config.max_position_embeddings) # 32768

# At inference: the rolling KV cache stays fixed at w=4096 entries
# regardless of how many tokens have been generated.
# This means constant memory even for very long generations.

# For a doc that exceeds the window, content from 4096+ tokens ago
# is no longer directly accessible — but through layer stacking,
# information still propagates if the model learned to do so.
Global Tokens and Hybrid Attention
Pure SWA cannot directly connect tokens more than w positions apart in a single layer. A common fix is to designate a few
global tokens

(such as [CLS] or a task prompt prefix) that attend to — and are attended by — every other token regardless of distance. Global tokens act as a communication bus, allowing any local token to influence any other token in two hops: local → global → target.

Mistral uses an alternating pattern: even layers use full attention, odd layers use SWA. This preserves global context in half the layers while saving memory overall. Gemma 2 adopts the same alternating scheme.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

def hybrid_attention(q, k, v, window: int, global_indices: list):
    """
    Sliding-window attention + global tokens.
    global_indices: positions that attend/are attended by all tokens.
    """
    B, T, d_k = q.shape
    scores = torch.bmm(q, k.transpose(1, 2)) / math.sqrt(d_k)  # (B, T, T)

    # Start with all positions masked
    mask = torch.zeros(T, T, dtype=torch.bool, device=q.device)

    # Allow causal local window for everyone
    for i in range(T):
        lo = max(0, i - window + 1)
        mask[i, lo:i+1] = True

    # Global tokens: bidirectional full attention
    for g in global_indices:
        mask[g, :g+1] = True   # global token attends all past
        mask[:, g]    = True   # all tokens can attend the global token

    scores = scores.masked_fill(~mask.unsqueeze(0), float('-inf'))
    attn = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1).nan_to_num()
    return torch.bmm(attn, v)

B, T, d_k, w = 1, 12, 16, 3
q = k = v = torch.randn(B, T, d_k)
# Position 0 is a [CLS]-style global token
out = hybrid_attention(q, k, v, window=w, global_indices=[0])
print(out.shape)  # (1, 12, 16)
When to use SWA:

If your task is primarily local (language modelling, code completion, chunked document summarisation), SWA with w around 2k–8k works well and enables much longer context within a fixed memory budget. If your task requires arbitrary cross-document references or global token comparisons, full attention or a hybrid with global tokens is safer. The window size is a hyperparameter: too small and long-range information is lost; too large and the O(T·w) savings evaporate.
What Sliding-Window Attention Is
Sliding-window attention (SWA) restricts each token to attending only to a fixed-size neighbourhood
of preceding tokens, replacing the O(n²) full-attention matrix with O(n·w) sparse attention
where w is the window size. Originally proposed in Longformer (Beltagy et al., 2020) and refined
in Mistral 7B (Jiang et al., 2023), SWA makes long-sequence inference practical on commodity hardware.
At layer ℓ, position i attends to positions [max(0, i−w+1) … i].
With a window of w=4 096 and 32 layers, the effective receptive field spans
over 131 000 tokens — enough to cover most practical contexts at a fraction of the memory cost.
Why It Matters
Transformer inference cost is dominated by the KV cache: with full attention a 32 B-parameter model
needs ~680 GB of KV cache for a 100 k-token batch at bfloat16. SWA caps the KV cache at
O(w · layers · d_model) regardless of sequence length, letting large models serve long documents
on a single GPU.
SWA also improves throughput: because each attention call touches only w keys instead of n,
GPU memory bandwidth is reduced proportionally. Mistral 7B outperforms LLaMA 13B on many benchmarks
while using less memory — an empirical demonstration that SWA does not sacrifice quality.
Benefits
Linear KV-cache growth in sequence length: O(w) instead of O(n) per layer.
Throughput improvement: attention FLOP per token is O(w·d) not O(n·d), enabling higher batch sizes.
Quality retention: receptive field grows linearly with layers, matching empirical long-range accuracy of full attention on standard benchmarks.
Rolling cache compatibility: only w key-value pairs need to be stored; oldest entries are evicted as the window slides.
Drop-in replacement: SWA layers are structurally identical to full-attention layers; switching is a config change.
Trade-offs
Hard receptive field ceiling: tokens beyond (w−1)·L positions cannot directly influence a position at layer L — very long-range dependencies require many layers or hybrid global tokens.
Irregular memory access: block-sparse patterns are slower than dense operations on current GPU hardware without a specialised kernel (e.g., Flash-Attention-2 or Triton sparse kernel).
Not universal: tasks that require comparing the first and last token of a document in a single layer (e.g., document-level coreference at shallow depth) degrade without global tokens.
Hyper-parameter sensitivity: window size must be tuned; too small collapses quality, too large erodes the memory savings.

attrs.alt: Sliding-window attention receptive field growth across layers
attrs.title: Sliding-window attention receptive field growth across layers

content.text: When to Use Sliding-Window Attention
Sequence length > 4 k tokens where full-attention KV cache would exhaust GPU memory.
Streaming / real-time inference where the rolling KV cache eviction is an architectural fit.
Models that already perform well with locality-biased attention (language, code, structured text).
Any setting where MHA VRAM is the bottleneck and you cannot switch to GQA alone.
Research prototypes testing efficient attention on consumer GPUs (24 GB VRAM, e.g., RTX 4090).
Real-World Example: Mistral 7B with SWA
The following script loads Mistral 7B (which ships with SWA enabled) and prints its window configuration.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
import torch

# Mistral 7B uses SWA with window_size=4096 and rolling KV cache
model_id = 'mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1'
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map='auto',
)

# SWA is transparent at inference — just pass a long context
long_doc = open('long_document.txt').read()
inputs = tokenizer(long_doc, return_tensors='pt').to(model.device)

with torch.no_grad():
    out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=256)

print(tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

# Inspecting the window config
cfg = model.config
print(f'sliding_window: {cfg.sliding_window}')  # 4096
print(f'num_hidden_layers: {cfg.num_hidden_layers}')  # 32
print(f'Receptive field: {cfg.sliding_window + (cfg.sliding_window - 1)*(cfg.num_hidden_layers - 1)}')
The receptive field after 32 layers is 4096 + (4096−1)×31 = 130 977 tokens — effectively unlimited for most documents.
Custom Sliding-Window Attention: Reference Implementation
A minimal PyTorch implementation for understanding (not for production — use Flash-Attention-2 kernels):

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

def sliding_window_attention(Q, K, V, window_size: int):
    # Q, K, V: (batch, heads, seq_len, d_head)
    B, H, T, D = Q.shape
    scale = math.sqrt(D)
    out = torch.zeros_like(Q)

    # Naive O(T*w) loop — illustrative only; use flash-attn in production
    for i in range(T):
        lo = max(0, i - window_size + 1)
        hi = i + 1  # causal: attend up to current position
        k_win = K[:, :, lo:hi, :]   # (B, H, w, D)
        v_win = V[:, :, lo:hi, :]   # (B, H, w, D)
        q_i   = Q[:, :, i:i+1, :]  # (B, H, 1, D)
        attn  = (q_i @ k_win.transpose(-2, -1)) / scale  # (B,H,1,w)
        attn  = F.softmax(attn, dim=-1)
        out[:, :, i:i+1, :] = attn @ v_win
    return out

# Quick sanity check
B, H, T, D = 1, 4, 16, 32
Q = torch.randn(B, H, T, D)
K = torch.randn(B, H, T, D)
V = torch.randn(B, H, T, D)
result = sliding_window_attention(Q, K, V, window_size=4)
print(result.shape)  # (1, 4, 16, 32)
Common Mistakes
Forgetting causal masking inside the window:
within the w-token window, autoregressive models still need the upper triangle masked. Omitting this leaks future tokens into the attention computation.
Setting w too small for the task:
a summarisation task that requires resolving pronouns across 10 k tokens needs at least log-depth stacking of windows; a single-layer window of 512 is insufficient.
Confusing window with context length:
Mistral 7B accepts up to 32 k tokens at inference, but its KV cache only stores the last 4 096 per layer — the model's context length and its window size are different concepts.
Not using a fused kernel:
naive Python loops implementing SWA are ~100× slower than an equivalent dense kernel; always use Flash-Attention-2 or a Triton sparse kernel for training.
Best Practices
Use Flash-Attention-2 with the sliding_window parameter rather than implementing sparse masking manually.
Pair SWA with a small number of global attention tokens (e.g., CLS, sentence boundaries) to allow coarse long-range interaction without full quadratic cost.
Match window size to your dominant task granularity: paragraph-level tasks work well with w=512–1 024; document tasks typically need w=4 096.
Monitor perplexity on long-range dependencies (e.g., PG19 book corpus) when tuning window size.
During export to ONNX or TensorRT, verify that the sparse masking is preserved; some exporters collapse the mask to dense full-attention.
Performance and Scalability
With a Triton or CUDA block-sparse kernel, SWA prefill throughput scales as O(n·w) vs O(n²) for full attention.
At n=32 k, w=4 k, this is an 8× reduction in attention FLOPs. KV cache memory is reduced from
O(n·L·d) to O(w·L·d) — a constant regardless of sequence length, so 128 k-token inference uses the
same cache memory as 4 k-token inference.

import time, torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

def throughput_benchmark(model_id, seq_lengths, batch=1, device='cuda'):
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_id, torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16, device_map=device
    ).eval()

    results = {}
    for seq_len in seq_lengths:
        input_ids = torch.randint(100, 30000, (batch, seq_len)).to(device)
        # warmup
        with torch.no_grad():
            _ = model(input_ids)
        torch.cuda.synchronize()
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        with torch.no_grad():
            _ = model(input_ids)
        torch.cuda.synchronize()
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
        results[seq_len] = {
            'latency_ms': elapsed * 1000,
            'tokens_per_sec': seq_len / elapsed,
        }
    return results

# Compare at various lengths — SWA latency grows linearly, not quadratically
# results = throughput_benchmark('mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1', [1024, 4096, 16384, 32768])
# for seq_len, r in results.items():
#     print(f'{seq_len:>6} tokens: {r["latency_ms"]:.1f} ms  ({r["tokens_per_sec"]:.0f} tok/s)')
Benchmarks on an A100-80 GB show Mistral 7B at 32 k tokens achieving ~2× higher throughput than
LLaMA 7B with full attention at the same sequence length, entirely due to the smaller KV footprint.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Flash-Attention-2 (Dao, 2023): native sliding_window parameter — the de-facto production kernel for SWA.
HuggingFace Transformers: MistralModel and MixtralModel expose sliding_window in their config; pass it at model load time.
vLLM: supports rolling KV cache for SWA models out-of-the-box, critical for continuous batching.
Triton: write custom block-sparse attention kernels for non-standard window shapes (e.g., dilated windows, strided patterns).
Comparison with Alternative Attention Mechanisms
Mechanism
KV Cache
Long-range
Implementation
Typical Use
Full (MHA)
O(n·L·d)
Perfect

Security and Reliability
SWA models can be probed with adversarial suffixes or prefixes designed to push malicious instructions
outside the attention window — effectively hiding the attack from later layers that process the generation.
Mitigations include system-prompt anchoring (pinning the system prompt as global tokens so it is always attended to)
and periodic re-injection of safety context at fixed intervals within the window.
On the reliability side, rolling KV eviction means that if the model is asked to reference text beyond
the window boundary, it cannot attend to it and may hallucinate. Production systems should chunk documents
with overlapping strides or use retrieval augmentation to ensure relevant context stays within the window.
Key Takeaways
SWA replaces O(n²) attention with O(n·w) by restricting each token's attention to a local window of size w.
Effective receptive field grows linearly with depth: receptive_field = w + (w−1)×(L−1).
KV cache memory is capped at O(w·L·d), making arbitrarily long contexts memory-feasible.
Flash-Attention-2 with sliding_window is the production path; naive Python loops are only for learning.
Global token hybrids (Longformer, BigBird, Mistral with sink tokens) recover long-range capability at minimal extra cost.
Mistral 7B demonstrated that SWA + GQA together allow a 7B model to outperform a 13B full-attention model on most benchmarks.
For production deployment, verify that rolling KV eviction logic is correctly implemented to prevent stale cache entries corrupting generations.
